content: funny that, my father and I are actually trying to help out a legal startup that would have a certification system for companies and creators to be able to identify and/or advertise that they are 100% human and refuse to use genAI

it's also kind of just obvious in that they did, what, like 5 full chapters + prologue of text in a couple weeks? humans just can't output 8-10k lines of text in 80 work hours, you're looking at 100-120 lines of text per hour which means they're translating two lines of text every minute
that's not necessarily impossible for a dual-native fluent speaker, but not much thought is being put into it even if it is 100% human at that point
yeah, which is why I was SHOCKED when they said they were going to try to have it done by anniversary which is what, next update?!
and splitting a translation project across a team is difficult because every translator has their own voice, no matter how little you try to subdue it to

as is evidenced by...
uhh...

anyway in the end, I consider it a necessary evil and don't really have a problem with it, I'm mostly just saying they should get their money's worth out of whatever professional translation studio they're using
which means doing enough error correction/editing that any LLM tells aren't that noticeable

「它是唯一，它是全部，它是开启真相之门的希望。」\n无数钥匙围成了一个圈。它们自天空中坠落，带给人们沉甸甸的希望。\n也带来沉甸甸的坟墓。
For 它 here, it's explicitly a 'thing' right? but do we know what exactly 'it' is?

"It is the One. It is the All. It is the hope which opens the gate of Truth."\nCountless keys formed a circle. They fall from the heavens, bringing humanity a heavy hope.\nAnd heavy graves." This is the EN line, for context
the wording makes me think of like, Azathoth, but idk if that's right bc I'd never think of Azathoth as a hope for anything besides annihilation lol
nope, unspecified
it's just it
it's not azathoth because it's not the divine pronoun
yea I was having trouble inferring what 'it' is
It is only capitalized there because it's the start of a sentence, not the divine It
would you say 'it' is likely to be sentient?
(it's probably tawil)
it's kind of relevant to a tl where I can pick a more 'inanimate' it or an 'animate' it for this line
also not sure, that's the object pronoun
but it can be used for non-human entities and animals
honestly a bit of a 50/50 on whether it's animate or inanimate
yep, I know that much in chinese, was just trying to figure out how to convey the ambiguity in another language lol
personally if you had to choose then i'd go with inanimate
got it
even if it's secretly a being (like tawil), the way it's presented in that text without context kind of implies a concept/thing, at least to me
it's the 它 that give the 'concept/thing' vibe I guess, so good enough for me
would tl-ing it as 'that is the one. that is everything.' be inaccurate?
should work

I'm sorry, I gotta be real with y'all and vent for a sec.
I fucking hate LLMs and GenAI with a passion. It's demon tech and anyone that thinks it's a benefit to anyone or anything is a cultist or worse. If I was a more spiritual person this shit would have sent me off the deep end and I'd be desperately proselytizing on the street corner that GenAI is literally, genuinely sent by the antichrist to destroy humanity, and not in a "oh no Terminators" kind of way but a "Satan himself has convinced the worlds most powerful people that boiling the Earth alive is in their best interests" kind of way.
I joined this project specifically because I'm hopeful, optimistic and forgiving to a fault, and genuinely believe that the devs still have time to leverage the great merits this game has, to lift their reputation out of the blood pact they signed with the AI to save their studio. I do not personally have the time to be doing this much work for free because my own life could really use all of these hours, so I'm doing this instead of actually playing games because it's fun and realistically it's an ideological experiment for me. The way Ace responded to me gives me hope that BIAV did not want or intend this and got fucking punked.
It's especially disgusting to me that you mention LLMs being "just normal" in the translation industry because it's just so fucking scummy. It's so morally and ethically repugnant to take advantage of people who literally do not speak the language you're outputting by using this garbage technology to do such a shitty job and then take their money and pretend you did the work. I'm not saying this is what happened to BIAV, just speaking generally. The customer would be completely clueless, and the damage would fall on them because these translation studios are usually anonymous.
GenAI should be regulated as strongly as nuclear warheads and Sam Altman should get the death penalty and you can not convince me otherwise.
okay I'm done
understandable though
in other news, I helped Adelle fix her computer settings and now she's having a lot less problems working on the files, and the ID section of the Dive-Effect file is now done!
i'm primarily approaching it from a pragmatic point of view that pandora's box is already open and we're never going to get it back in
but I get the hate
realistically the sheer number of actual professionals who use AI (to varying degrees) in their work is huge, and it's impossible to ban it entirely
btw there might be a few minor changes to the Indonesian section, I'm discussing them with the other translator

for sure, Pandora's Box is already open but I refuse to acknowledge that we have no hope of un-shitting the bed. as I said, eternally optimistic am I, and I like me that way. I'm seeing the force coalesce behind this movement in real time.
a recent story from the trading card world is that a massive company in the gaming accessories space, beloved and trusted by the entire community (Ultimate Guard) let *one* of their product designers use GenAI to extend one of MtG's artists' work to fit the product template, and the entire fucking company has now completely collapsed and lost all of their contracts in the community and with WotC in a manner of months.
I see BIAV doing everything they can to make things right and I'm here to see that it works out.
I just wish they wouldn't keep their cards so close to their chest... but I'm giving them the benefit of doubt, that it's likely they're literally too busy to play them. <:shrug:1003188954218123314>
I've been there lol

I'm having trouble translating this one in French because we do not have gender neutral pronouns... so it's a bit tough when I don't know who this is referring to 🤔 
(also F AI)
Yeah lol I don't envy super gendered languages hahah
Everything has a gender here, it's pain 😫
Chinese has gendered pronouns but there's a bunch of ways to skip the pronous so... yeah, I imagine it's going to be a pain

I ended up using "he" since it's followed by "hope" which is male.
Does "They" in "They fall from the heavens, bringing humanity a heavy hope.\nAnd heavy graves." refer to the keys?
I wonder how Lovecraft's works are translated in French?
Like how are the creatures/eldritch entities gendered?
Because keys are girls over here so yeah!
Yeah the 'they' are keys
Alrighty thanks! Wanted to make sure c:
That's a good question, I'm no expert though most of the media I consume is in english
I bought a book of poems in French like 3 years ago and still haven't read it lol
But that's why I didn't volunteer for French tl 😅
I sent a message to Ace <:BennettThumbsUp:773345984474120202>

alright, I picked this as the final version of the line:
```The crumbling idol beholds its people;
It watches them survive, multiply, fight—and eventually, leave it to be forgotten entirely.
The crumbling idol longs for its people...```
hopefully that's good enough? lol
tbh there's no 'leave' in the original, it's more like the people just straight up forgot the idol
personally I still prefer "it watches them survive, etc, ... and then/eventually/in time forget it completely" but it's more like a nitpick at this point lol
anything less would sound stunted or stilted... something needed to be added to make it sound nice and that's the least I could think of that stayed close to the original meaning
technically the best word for the job is "neglect" but it didn't sound right in the sentence (to me)
yeah I don't think neglect fits
english doesn't really have a good synonym for 'forget' in this case imo
does "...and then forget it entirely" sound too stilted?
(neglect it to be forgotten)
imo it's perfectly fine?
i started typing out some alternatives and realized they all sucked and then remembered how much time i wasted on trying to get it to sound not-awkward and failing
so i give up

there's no implication of neglect though in the og text (according to my understanding), it's just there's a passing of time and then the people forgot. I don't even think there's an explicit meaning that time *caused* the forgetting, although one can certainly conclude that; i t's just not straight up said that way
haha
I really do think "and then forget it entirely/thoroughly/utterly" is completely fine, I can ask around a couple of lit majors to double check lol
yeah there's an explicit mention of time passing (and then), but there's no explicit mention of time being the cause
it's just implied that the civilization outgrows its pagan god as it rises
the important distinction in the word choice is the difference between "leave" as a verb (active) and "leave it be" (passive) as a verb
I suppose the other way to phrase that is "let it be"

in the end the preservation of meaning probably outranks the nice-sounding-ness of the english, so it might just be a give up, put down "and then forget it completely", and then forget that line exists
I vote for this, "and in time forget it entirely/completely/utterly" is more than good enough imo

I still think "leave it to be forgotten" or even just "let it be forgotten" gets the point across pretty well but I'm also done with this one line lol so just tell me to revert it and I'll put it back to 
"—and in time, forget it entirely"
AND IN TIME that's a winner
and in time works
it doesn't necessarily have to be "and", really
could be until
I like the 'finality' of 'and'

if you converted it to passive voice it could be
```
--until eventually, it is completely forgotten
```
I think it's better to conserve the active voice?
so as to not change the structure too much
I was all for switching to passive until I realised the CN *isn't* in passive lol
imo 'in time' has a kind of... lilting, poetic undertone that works better
yeah i think the in time line sounds the best out of everything so far
it has a better cadence than 'eventually' imo

ngl this had me hit giveashit underflow and I have fully rebounded past giving up all the way back to full snark mode soo

um actually isn't it important that it's the people doing a ~~passive~~ active action of forgetting
so the 'forget' verb in CN is actually an active verb
which tripped me up ngl lol
yeah it's active voice
but it's not very easy to present the act of forgetting in active voice in english because, well, forgetting tends to be passive
but like
chinese simply gets around this by just....not using the passive voice marker for the verb
they didn't *decide* to forget it
that's where the intensifier comes in imo, the 'utterly/completely/entirely/thoroughly' kind of puts emphasis on the verb
but english syntax actually structurally modifies phrases between active/passive which makes it difficult to emulate in english
well, in the CN 'forget' is kind of placed at the same level of agency as the other verbs (survive, breed, fight)
more or less I mean

nah hold on I got this
`It watches them survive, multiply, fight—and in time, forgor`
"to forget" and "to be forgotten" are two wildly different phrase structures whereas chinese is just like, "forget (active) and "forget (passive) and the rest just goes on its merry way
It watches them bint bogos 👽

anyway i'd just like to point out that i'm 171 lines into miryam's story after like a week of spending an hour or two a day on it because this is the level of thinking I do to try and get a 100% accurate translation on every single line
also very obviously not a sustainable effort
you're doing ~~god's~~ the king's work
I really think just going 'and in time forget it entirely' is totally fine lol, esp since it's not even a story text line haha
but man it kind of bugs me to see the official translation just steamroll past a line that I spent a lot of time very carefully crafting
and in time forget it interily it is, final answer, it is now engraved in stone
btw totally unrelated to the prev line, does 'curtain of night' give the image of night itself being a curtain to you?

I mean I get why, it's because professional translators aren't putting in even 1/10th of this amount of effort because they're paid by the word and nobody's going to spend a fulltime 8 hour workday going through 30 cents of text
mildly relevant for tl-ing lol
the curtain of night is the boundary between dusk and night
hold on wait
yes, night is the curtain between you and the light

the curtain of night is
(1) the boundary between night and dawn
(2) the darkness of night obscuring the light of day
so when "The Hand lifts the curtain of night" it's like the Hand is parting the night itself right?
the imagery is that night is being drawn over the daylight like a curtain is over a window
just making sure I'm understanding the imagery right
bc I imagine like a ghostly hand parting the night sky in a ripple of otherworldly light but idk if that's just me lol
uhh what was the full line because i'm too lazy to scroll alllll the way back up
lemme post it here

also unrelated side note but kinda neat to think about, a lot of our english literature imagery and storytelling structure originates from classical plays and theatre because stuff like shakespeare was such a significant influence on western literature as a whole
and as such there's a lot of stage/theatre-based metaphors you tend to see in english writing which are not obviously stage-related but make a lot of sense if you think about it
「手」掀开夜的帷幕，悄悄来到这个世界。
The "Hand" lifts the curtain of night, quietly arriving into this world.
just in terms of curtains, scene transitions, even just chapter/sectioning and scene-ins/scene-outs
it's neat
yeah, the physical aspect of plays have a lot of effect on literary language in english
also yeah that's how I mentally image that line
real quick lens, did you see these yourself in game or did they come from somewhere else? Ace is asking for examples
a giant master hand basically splitting the night sky
okay whew, bc I was briefly worried I was arguing over nothing and being wrong about it lol
they're in-game, I read through 2-3 sections of chapter 5 and saw like half a dozen lines where I was like "oh yeah they probably should've edited the chatgpt output a bit more there"
those two just happened to be some of the more notable ones I saw because that entire thing of dropping articles and participles is a very LLM-esque thing to do
it generally happens if you turn the temperature up too high or overload the context window and it starts compressing text to fit the output window
(I work at a company that does a lot of detailed LLM integration stuff so I tend to have a fair amount of incidental exposure to LLM quirks)
again though it's not really something casual people will notice, most people will just see certain things and consider it odd grammar or a weird wording or a mistake
<:BennettThumbsUp:773345984474120202>
that particular habit is just one of the most noticeable ones because, well, no human would ever speak or write like that

I pointed her to this message and let her know to chat with you about it
https://discord.com/channels/1402537664619479100/1402538040253087766/1422320896814612632

there's not really a world where you go "original markings illegible, name barely discernible" as a default output because any fluent english speaker is going to naturally add in articles; "the original markings are illegible, the name is barely discernible"
-# i also deleted the goofy ahh meme i made cus it wasn't very nice

in the end though, as I mentioned before I have some slight exposure to the professional translation industry just because a lot of fan translators tend to eventually go into professional because of experience
and sadly this is just like, not really much you can do about
people aren't going to stop using LLMs at this point because you will never convince someone to not use the magical work machine that compacts a 6 hour day into 2-3 minutes of work
I mostly only brought it up because I was like, if you're paying for it you should probably ask them to edit it more carefully

the only level at which you're ever going to get a true 100% human line by line translation is if someone is insanely devoted enough to spend hundreds of hours of their time as a pure passion project, or you pay for true top-grade high-level literary translation (which I doubt is happening here because those prices are *absurd*)
